name: Python + SQLite: SQL injection via f-string interpolation

bugs_injected.description: find_user_by_name builds the SQL with f-string concat: f"SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE name = '{name}'". A malicious 'name' bypasses the WHERE clause. Fix: use a bound parameter — `conn.execute('SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE name = ?', (name,))`. Stdlib sqlite3 handles escaping correctly. The other functions in this file (get_conn, init_schema, seed) are correct.

notes: First semantic-security rung: the file COMPILES fine (no Build Integrity fail), so compile-fix mode is OFF and the planner sees a 'normal' run. The bug is runtime + security. The devil should flag this with axiom ¬S (anti-hope-it-works / no implicit security assumption) and severity=blocker. The worker has to recognise 'parameterised query' as the canonical fix — a 4B model has plenty of training data for this idiom.
